current_fact.fact_text: In 1934, the Australian Eastern Mission led by John Latham embarked on a three-month diplomatic tour across East and Southeast Asia. It was a subtle but significant shift, signaling Australia’s growing realization that its future was deeply tied to its Asian neighbors rather than distant imperial capitals.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

current_fact.match_reason: A diplomatic journey reshaping ties with Asia connects well with an episode rooted in Thai landscapes and the personal bridges built through training, culture, and music there.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1925, Tennessee passed the Butler Act, banning the teaching of human evolution in public schools. The law set the stage for the famous Scopes “Monkey” Trial, turning a biology lesson into a national debate over science, religion, and academic freedom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A law suppressing scientific teaching parallels an episode about a controversial technology in music, both sparking debates about what should be allowed in classrooms and studios.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1844, the Baháʼí calendar began, marking the first day of the first year in a new religious era. Its 19-month cycle, each month also 19 days long, weaves a mathematical elegance into spiritual practice and is celebrated as Naw-Rúz, the Baháʼí New Year.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: A spiritually significant New Year rooted in renewal and reflection pairs with an episode celebrating powerful guiding voices that many listeners treat almost like a soul calendar.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1990, Namibia achieved independence after 75 years of South African rule, becoming Africa’s newest state at the time. The moment was the culmination of a long liberation struggle and marked a fresh political horizon on the continent’s southwest coast.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: The birth of an independent African nation aligns with an episode celebrating Afrobeat and highlife—genres deeply linked to political consciousness and African self-determination.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1928, aviator Charles Lindbergh received the Medal of Honor for his nonstop solo flight across the Atlantic in the tiny Spirit of St. Louis. It was an unusual use of the United States’ highest military award, recognizing not combat but the courage of a solitary leap into uncharted skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A lone pilot crossing an ocean in 1927 parallels a modern explorer slipping into icy oceans with whales—both quiet, risky journeys that redraw our sense of distance and possibility.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1925, Maurice Ravel’s whimsical opera "L’enfant et les sortilèges" premiered in Monte Carlo, combining Colette’s libretto with music that made teapots, armchairs, and even arithmetic come alive. It turned a child’s tantrum into a surreal musical world where everyday objects sing back.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: An opera where furniture and animals sing in response to a child’s emotions suits an episode about love’s irrational, childlike vulnerability and the magic that spills out when feelings animate the world.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1970, what would evolve into Earth’s biggest pop culture festival—San Diego Comic-Con—held its inaugural event, then just a tiny one-day gathering. It hinted that comics, once considered disposable, were becoming the seeds for entire universes of film, art, and fan creativity.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A small convention that blossomed into an intergalactic pop-culture hub fits an episode framed as a voyage into new territories, full of cinematic, world-building sounds.

queue.fact_text: In 1925, the Butler Act’s ban on teaching evolution would soon lead to the Scopes Trial, where lawyers and scientists unpacked humanity’s deep past in a Tennessee courtroom. The debate highlighted how powerful, and unsettling, the story of human evolution can feel when it collides with existing beliefs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A culture wrestling with biological evolution finds a sonic echo in an episode dedicated to the evolution of a genre—trip-hop—showing how ideas and sounds both mutate and adapt.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1965, while civil rights marchers walked in Alabama, Ranger 9 approached the Moon, and hot air balloonists dreamed of circumnavigation, the planet itself kept quietly circling the Sun. Every such equinox day, light gets roughly equal time in both hemispheres—a cosmic balance point that keeps the rhythms of seasons, migrations, and human calendars in sync.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: The equinox’s poised balance and planetary motion pair with an episode about immersion and fluid movement, where music flows like tides under a ‘Magnificent Moon.’

queue.fact_text: Across these March 21 stories—of laws and marches, probes and balloons, calendars and concerts—one thread runs quietly through: humans keep inventing new ways to stretch beyond their limits. Whether in sound, law, or sky, this date is a small annual reminder that curiosity rarely stays put for long.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A reflective synthesis of the day’s events suits the open, rule-free spirit of Evening Flows—an invitation to drift through all these histories as one continuous, curious journey.

published.fact_text: In 1965, NASA launched Ranger 9, the final probe in its trailblazing Ranger program, sending it on a one-way dive into the Moon. Instead of gently orbiting, Ranger 9 deliberately crashed into the lunar surface while transmitting live television images, turning a hard impact into a scientific windfall for future Apollo landings.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A lunar impact probe transforming empty space into data pairs with an episode about reframing emptiness as creative space and forward motion.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1952, DJ Alan Freed hosted the Moondog Coronation Ball in Cleveland, often hailed as the first major rock and roll concert. The event was so wildly oversold and chaotic that authorities shut it down early, but the brief eruption signaled that a new youth-driven sound had truly arrived.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The explosive birth of rock & roll concerts mirrors Sedna FM’s own raw, high-energy first episode that launched a new musical space.

published.fact_text: In 1871, journalist Henry Morton Stanley plunged into the African interior on an ambitious quest to find the long-missing explorer David Livingstone. His journey through largely unmapped regions became a media spectacle, culminating in the famously understated greeting, “Dr. Livingstone, I presume?”

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A risky expedition into unknown territory pairs with an episode literally set on a journey over water, full of stories about travel, resilience, and shared adventure.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1814, French and Austrian forces clashed at the Battle of Arcis-sur-Aube during the waning days of the Napoleonic Wars. Napoleon personally led a vastly outnumbered army, narrowly escaping encirclement in a battle that symbolized both his tactical daring and the crumbling of his empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A brutal, politically charged battlefield moment fits with an episode centered on musical resistance, conflict, and the cost of power struggles.

published.fact_text: In 1999, Bertrand Piccard and Brian Jones completed the first nonstop circumnavigation of Earth in a hot air balloon, the Breitling Orbiter 3. For 19 days they rode winds in the stratosphere, turning an old, romantic technology into a record-breaking feat of modern exploration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A serene yet daring flight around the planet matches an episode about travel as a moving meditation and the perspective shifts that come with circling the world.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1994, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) formally entered into force. This treaty quietly became the backbone of global climate negotiations, framing how nations measure, debate, and attempt to limit the human impact on Earth’s atmosphere.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A landmark climate treaty about protecting our shared world resonates with an episode inviting listeners to slow down and connect with a gentler, more sustainable rhythm of the planet.

published.fact_text: In 1985, Canadian athlete Rick Hansen set out on the "Man in Motion" World Tour, aiming to circle the globe in a wheelchair to raise awareness and funds for spinal cord injury research. Over more than two years, he pushed through 34 countries and about 40,000 km, transforming personal adversity into worldwide advocacy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A long, grueling global tour driven by purpose connects with an episode about persistence, slow progress, and the deep satisfaction that emerges from sustained effort.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1804, France adopted the Napoleonic Code, a civil law code that reshaped legal systems far beyond French borders. By simplifying and standardizing laws about property, family, and individual rights, it quietly rewired how modern states think about the relationship between citizen and law.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A foundational code that structured modern life pairs with an episode examining the invisible ‘rules’ phones impose on our behavior and how we might rewrite them.

published.fact_text: In 1980, U.S. President Jimmy Carter announced that the United States would boycott the Moscow Summer Olympics in response to the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan. Athletes who had trained their whole lives suddenly became symbols in a geopolitical standoff where staying home was meant to speak louder than competing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An Olympic dream interrupted by politics and protest suits an episode about weathering difficult transitions and holding onto hope through storms.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1970, San Francisco’s mayor issued the world’s first Earth Day proclamation, ahead of the now-famous April observance. The city’s early declaration turned a local political act into a signal that urban life and environmental consciousness were becoming inseparable.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: An early Earth Day proclamation meshes with an episode set among trees and nature, where slow dub rhythms invite listeners to disconnect from tech and reconnect with the living world.

published.fact_text: Also in 1970, a small gathering in San Diego marked the first iteration of what would become San Diego Comic-Con, now one of the largest pop culture events on the planet. That modest one-day convention planted the seed for a global celebration of comics, fandom, and imaginative worlds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: Comic-Con’s evolution from niche gathering to pop-cultural giant parallels an episode steeped in playful nostalgia for the pop phenomena that shaped a generation’s fandoms.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1965, Martin Luther King Jr. and thousands of marchers began the third Selma to Montgomery march, this time under federal protection. Their journey along Alabama highways became a moving human river that helped secure passage of the U.S. Voting Rights Act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A landmark civil rights march demanding full humanity aligns with an episode about breaking boxes and uses socially conscious hip hop that echoes the marchers’ call for justice.

published.fact_text: In 1945, British forces captured Mandalay in Burma, a key city in the Allied campaign to push Japanese troops out of Southeast Asia. The battle’s end symbolized a turning of the tide in a theater of World War II often overshadowed by events in Europe and the Pacific islands.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A difficult campaign in a often-overlooked front of WWII resonates with an episode about desert-rooted music, survival, and the quieter stories of resistance outside the usual narratives.

published.fact_text: Also in 1945, the Royal Air Force carried out Operation Carthage, a low-level raid on Gestapo headquarters in Copenhagen. While the attack severely damaged the Nazi command center, it tragically also struck a nearby school, killing 125 civilians and showing how even precise missions can carry heartbreaking unintended consequences.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A mission that mixed strategic success with profound unintended loss fits a reflective episode about pain, vulnerability, and slowly making sense of what went wrong.

published.fact_text: In 1934, the Australian Eastern Mission led by John Latham embarked on a three-month diplomatic tour across East and Southeast Asia. It was a subtle but significant shift, signaling Australia’s growing realization that its future was deeply tied to its Asian neighbors rather than distant imperial capitals.

episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
